Label: Technical
Data:
This might be a solution for players suffering from low fps in fullscreen mode despite having a decent pc. Low fps are only present in fullscreen mode, once switching to windowed it is butter smooth.

I had this problem and tried everything. Eventually I found a solution myself and I want to share it here so that other people can try it as well and report back.

My problem was caused by AMD FreeSync. I have it enabled for all games but it seems that DST has a problem with it. Open Adrenaline software, goto Gaming, then open the profile for DST and on the right side below "Display" disable AMD FreeSync.

With this setting I have the same fps in fullscreen as in windowed mode.

Hope this helps other players.

Label: Technical
Data:
Greetings!
Congratulations with the recent release, which all we waited for a long time. :)
I like to play DST with my friends. And I found the map sharing and global players indicators as the highly requested feature. I know that this is not a feature of the vanilla game, but there is an excellent and very popular mod, which provides the corresponding functionality. Unfortunately, according to the mod's author @rezecib, map sharing became broken at some point of game development because of some changes in the game's core if I understood correctly. And it still remains not fixed. Again, according to the author, there is not a very big change needed to make map sharing working...
Dear devs, please make the corresponding fixes to the game's core so that map sharing become able to work again! Especially if it is not a very big deal...
Perhaps not many players write about it here on the forums, but rest assured that this is a very requested feature. Many users of the mod ask about availability of the feature for very long time! Even though the author clearly noted the details about the unavailability of the feature on the mod's page. As for me and my friends: we stopped playing the game some time ago mostly due to the fact that the map sharing is broken. :( And we hoped that it will be fixed at some point near the release of the game. But the game has been released and map sharing is still not there... :(
Make us happy! :) Thanks in advance.

Label: Experiential
Data:
Hey hey hey! I've seen everyone has already explained what it is, BUT. Few more notes! If you like the location, you can move like a screen or two away from the nests, base there, and you should be fine. Moose goose is not strictly hostile, it is neutral, unless you're very close. She will try hopping closer to you when you're nearby, though, so be careful! The plus of basing nearby is... they can help you against the hounds! Or frogs. If you run around them with hounds, sooner or later the hounds will drop the agrro and attack the goose. When that happens, they're basically done for, lol. And frogs... there's a certain thing during spring that can cause tons of them to spawn - you can despawn them by getting away, but why do that if..... You can get the frogs to attack the moose goose! And trust me, you wanna do that, lol. Frogs just attack things around, and sooner or later one will attack the moose goose if you haven't attacked any frog before (otherwise they might be too occupied with attacking you, lol). If you wanna tackle one or two of these beasts (the moose goose and ESPECIALLY the kids), their loot is highly valuable, as it's used for crafting of some very useful tools (weather pain) if you want to tackle on more complicated bosses while solo! Can also make a biiiig fan, used to l extinguish nearby fires (not that effective, but good enough in a pinch and on the go!) or cool down, for example during summer!

If you do decide to fight them, or the frogs do, be careful about the young ones! As others have said, their attack (which goes far in a strraaaaaaight line) destroys any buildings in their path!
Notes: Notes: Helpful tips on how to deal with game boss, Goose

Label: Experiential
Data:
Just keep moving and don’t stay in the same place too long (watch for tentacles), and you should be just fine. In and out, follow the road as much as possible, get what you need, and then leave ASAP. I don’t really “like” being in the swamp either, but I’ve learned to love it for certain resources, and I try not to spend any more time there than one (in-game) day at a time, and never camp there overnight if I don’t have to.

If there’s a safe space on the edge of a swamp biome, I usually like to make a little camp there with like just a fire/crockpot/chest so I have somewhere to rest if things get dire.

Label: Experiential
Data:
100% the easiest way for them to start is a solo game. Either trying it out themselves, or with you watching and guiding them. Playing a world together when they’re brand new will be frustrating for both of you.

You won’t be able to have time to explain much of anything if you’re trying to do anything yourself, let alone prep a base and food etc for two people. And if you do a bunch of stuff without them, they won’t have learned how to make the basic things cause it’s already done.

The biggest thing, is that it’s much more tolerable to pause often, especially the auto pause setting for the crafting menu in solo player. The hardest thing about being new is feeling in a constant rush, and not being able to take your time and look at what you can make, and how.

If you’re not there in person, and just giving advice on how to learn… tell them to use the wiki! When they die to something, or discover something they want to know, Google what it is, or how to solve it.

Secondly, explain the rollback feature! In the game if you die, pause, select server, rollback. Can redo up to the last 5 days.

Label: Experiential
Data: People who join public servers usually just want to play for a while. Some are base builders, others rushers. You'll almost always see people rushing countless things on a public server. I'd say the true experience of playing the game is doing it in coordination with someone, in a voice chat, progressing together in whatever way you prefer. As the game's title suggests, its best feature is its ability to create combinations and other elements thanks to being multiplayer. Of course, this doesn't mean you can't still play single-player.

Label: Experiential
Data:
I've got all the different games. I prefer DST even to play solo. I've never jumped into any public server. Either I play my own private server opened to my friends, or I will play my other server where I play all by myself. There's much more content throughout the game and quality of life updates for me to keep playing. And the fact characters have skilltrees is honestly so cool. I'm yet to try everyone's new skill trees. Original DS is amazing (and my favourite is Shipwrecked by a long way) but I've played recently again and I miss stuff like the crafting menu for example.

Anyway, all that to say, I play so much DST but I have never ever joined public servers. Playing solo on DST is still very fun. There's so much to do. The caves/ruins, the ocean, all the bosses, etc. It's just harder than basegame as the bosses are scaled for multiplayer plays (Dragonfly and Bee Queen have about 28k HP each for example). It's not undoable solo however, it just requires lots of preparation ahead of time. For Bee Queen, in my server with friends, they were doing other stuff while I've soloed her like twice by myself. I just created a massive death trap of tentacles and let her slowly die from tentacle slaps lol. It just took a really long time for me to set it up.

Label: Strategy
Data:
I suggest learning how to fight spiders, as it can help solve both problems. 6 silk can make a top hat which does wonders for sanity restoring, as well as dropping monster meat. Monster meat, along with like, 3 berries in a crock pot, can really provide a LOT more hunger than their ingredients separately.
Or like someone else said, wear armor and get some cactus to cook over a fireplace. That solves sanity and hunger too.

Label: Strategy
Data:
Farming spiders will fix all of these problems and open up a lot of options. They will offer food, healing items, and silk. Silk can solve sanity with top hats and tents.

Spiders can be moved if you destroy a tier 3 nest and replant the eggs. Be careful that a tier 3 nest can become a miniboss. Planting too many spiders nearby can become a headache fast. If fighting them becomes overwhelming, use the same traps that you guys are already using for rabbits, as they can trap the spiders too.

Silk also opens up a lot of new food options with bug nets and fishing poles. Honey can refill all three of the player's stats and is very efficient. I recommend bee boxes as they are one of my favorite options.

If you are eating crockpot foods that restore 10-15 sanity, and wearing a top hat when you arent fighting, you'll find sanity easier to manage. Sleeping will restore large amounts of sanity and health at the cost of hunger.

Label: Strategy
Data: Actually, its the "prototype" new recipe that gives a flat +15 sanity. When there's a lightbulb icon, it's mean you can unlock a new recipe.

Label: Strategy
Data:
For sanity, don’t neglect sleep. A lot of people just stand around and wait out the night but actually sleeping does help (unless Wickerbottom as she can’t sleep). Crafting a flower garland helps significantly too and is much easier to get than the top hat.

Don’t pick every single flower; leave a couple in an area so that the bees can pollinate them (spawns more flowers). Ideally, put a few bee boxes near your flower patch too. Will help with pollination and the honey you’ll get from the bees boosts all 3 stats.

For food - place rabbit traps directly over rabbit holes for more success catching them. Removes the need to put down bait or the risk of them getting scared off.

That said, rabbits can be a bit of a new player food trap, they can be too unreliable. Cactus from desert biome will help you a lot and they regrow fast, even in winter. Bird cage will allow you to get eggs (you can feed monster meat, seeds, etc to the bird to convert it to eggs so it’s more usable). As others said, make sure you’re using a crockpot not just cooking over a fire.

Dig up and replant berry bushes near your base (you’ll need to re-fertilise them with rot or fertiliser).

Also, there’s no shame in adjusting a few settings if it gives you guys a more enjoyable experience. Turning up the regrowth & spawn rate of flowers a little makes a big difference :)

Label: Strategy
Data:
1: science machine -> crackpot; it's a structure to combine 4 foods into one with a recipe; get monster meat from spiders and any filling (berries, carrot, ice, cooked chestnuts) 1 piece of meat and 3 fillings make meatballs that fill Wilson by half

2: have an armor (science machine -> alchemy engine -> football helmet or log suit), find either desert, pick up cactus flesh, cook it on a fire, huge sanity food

3, health; some very common health recipes are 1 butterfly wing, 1 vegetable (like cactus flesh), 2 twigs -> butterfly muffin; have a birdcage (requires alchemy engine and 8 reeds you pick in the swamp, plus seeds and gold), trap a bird (bird trap requires silk from spiders and sticks) feed it monster meat, it returns eggs; 1 egg, 1 meat (any), 1 vegetable (cactus flesh), 1 filler (berry, ice, not a stick) -> pierogi, gives a ton of health

But the most important thing is to stay nomad until you've explore all the map, so you know where everywhere is. You're going to struggle if you are limited to the resources of just one biome.

A lot of resources are movable, replanted berry bushes and grass will require fertilizing when you plant them, but it can be worth it to make a good base. There's also plenty of valuable structures like beeboxes.

Label: Strategy
Data:
DST Basics

HUNGER: most people lose 75/day, be aware if yours is different. Eat efficiently--don't constantly hover at 80%+ hunger -> let hunger reach ~0 -> slam high-hunger food. No eating before Day 2

PRIMARY RESOURCES: 40 grass, 40 twigs. 2 grass+2 twigs -> torch -> burn trees -> charcoal + night light -> keep working/moving -> darkness won't kill you

ALSO COLLECT: 20 logs, 20 flint, 40 rocks. Flint -> basic pickaxe, axe, spear for weapon. Target boulders with gold lines for 1-2 gold each -> science machine/alchemy engine -> stronger tools to replace flint. You need gold more than nitre (plain boulders)

HAMMER PIG HOUSES: easiest+fastest way to get lots of boards, cut stone, pig skin early on

1st SCIENCE MACHINE + PROTOTYPING: backpack, boards, cut stone, rope, doodad, alchemy engine, bug net, shovel, spear, beefalo bell -> easy big meat

1st NIGHTS: collect resources while holding torch OR burn a tree -> chop/mine/collect freely and easily, as well as catch 1 fireflies -> miner hat -> portable, refillable light on headslot

KEEP BURNING TREES: you'll want 32-62 charcoal (4-8 crock pots, 4-7 drying racks) for a starter kitchen

KEEP MOVING: especially at night! You should aim to map out your world's edges by Day 12-13 to find all mainland biomes. If wildfires on -> base in the oasis. If wildfires off (I recommend) -> base where there's quick enough access to points of interest such as: sinkhole (light bulbs -> miner hat/lantern +19%), wormhole (fast transport), beefalo (food source+protection from hounds) pig king (infinite gold, big+small meat options)

SANITY: having 0 sanity will not kill you. Pick a flower (+5 sanity), prototype (+15 sanity), dried meat (+10-15 sanity)

KILLING NIGHTMARES: there are 2 nightmares. 1 moves slow, 1 moves fast. BOTH have TINY attack range. Fighting: they come to you -> take small step back as they attack -> hold F to attack until they spawn away -> come to you -> dodge -> hold F -> repeat till dead. Whoever gets the kill regains some sanity. Learn to be comfortable fighting them so you can sooner ignore taking care of sanity (until you get strong sanity items)

ARMOR: log suit+football helmet as your main armor. Use log suit and miner hat to fight comfortably at night

STARTER BASE: kitchen -> ice box surrounded by 4-8 crock pots so you can access all pots without closing ice box -> cook multiple things at once -> cook efficiently. 4-7 drying racks next to crock pots area

DO NOT BUILD: walls -> easily destroyed by hostile mobs, expensive to repair. chests -> only make chests for items that moleworms steal (rocks, flint, gems, etc), items pigs/spiders can eat (Deerclops eyeball, meat, pig skin, etc). Much quicker to find items out laid out neatly on the ground -> use pitchfork to dig turf and place carpet under your items -> spring lureplants won't steal your items

BEEFALO: beefalo bell -> bond -> relocate beefalo anywhere -> reproduce in spring. Keep 1-2 herds near base for easy meat. Keep spare beefalo bell for hunting -> bond to beefalo -> drag away from herd -> hit×1 -> dodge -> hit×5 -> dodge -> repeat till dead (4 big meat, beefalo wool, chance for beefalo horn -> best winter hat)

WALRUS CAMPS: winter -> approach walrus camp -> chase walrus (beware ice hounds) -> he stops running -> you kill -> repeat till you have Walrus Tusk (for walking cane(+25% speed)) and Tam o' Shanter (+6.6 sanity/min+warmth).

DEERCLOPS: runs over trees -> logs $$$. When ready to kill her -> run in -> do 3-4 hits -> run directly away -> repeat till dead, eat jerky (+10-15 sanity)

HEALING: kill butterflies -> +8 HP, cook pierogi/dragonpie -> +40 HP

SCIENCE TIERS: if you have an alchemy engine -> hammer science machine. If you have a shadow manipulator -> hammer prestihatitator

OTHER: F = attack hostile mobs, Ctrl+F = attack neutral/passive mobs

GET MODS: geometric placement+drop -> let you place items+structures neatly and accurately on a grid

ADAPTATION: this game takes time to adjust to. You will improve, just be patient and practice

Label: Strategy
Data:
Either memorize a lot of recipes or use some combination of full hunger restoring foods - plenty of options for this but Meaty Stew+one other decent food(Like Meatballs) goes a decent way. You can also just power through it with high value individual foods if you have the resources, like just eating Meaty Stews until you're full, and then eating after the penalty has worn off.

Set up an Automatic Volt Goat farm early - this can get you plenty of passive Volt Goat Horns early. You do need to go to the Lunar Isle to get an Anenemy to set one up, but it's not too difficult to manage and you can also pilfer the other resources while you're there(Stone Fruit makes for great and easy filler, along with Kelp, both of which you can get there).

If you're unlucky with random seeds for specific crops, you can save them until a specific season - the wiki has a table, but Pepper is most common in Summer(3.2% chance), compared to Autumn(2.7% chance), Winter(1.7% chance) and Spring(1.3% chance). This can help you try to target farm the crops if you're willing to wait a little bit and are just having bad luck randomly planting so it can maximize your chances.

Powdercake(Corn, Honey and 2 Twigs) is a food dish with a hugely long spoilage time(10k+ days) and no benefits for eating(I guess -3 Health is a 'benefit'), but you can spice it. Helpful if you don't have Bundling Wrap yet and want to spice food for you or your team to use later, and spicing active food would result in it not being useful.

Healing foods are less effective to spam, so I like getting Bee Boxes set up for Honey Poultice and Honey Ham/Honey Nuggets - multiple healing options are nice to have, and the food is pretty simple to source since it just takes Meats/Filler(That you likely already have/want) in addition to the Honey, which also helps the whole diet thing. If you suck at combat this can give you healing options after you disengage, and Honey Poultice is definitely one of the better, stronger healing sources even if it has a relatively high cost(At least until you get Monkeytails or unless you base in the Swamp).

I think if you're playing Warly solo, he doesn't really shine quite as much as other characters - he's an effective force multiplier on a team, and really welcome to have since it both spreads damage out to the team(Minimizing his downside of being difficult to rapidly heal) while also ensuring fights go much quicker with his buffs... which is sort of unfortunate, and makes some of the team based tips much less relevant.
Notes:
Notes:
Tips and tricks for character Warly

Label: Strategy
Data: One of the most pervasive DST myths is that meteor fields grow and shift. They do not. In reality, meteor biomes have a set number of meteor spawners, which have a radius of 15 tiles. To make sure you’re safe from meteors, you should always base at least 15 tiles from the edge of the meteor biome (including over water) since that’s the absolute max distance a meteor can fall. I’m guessing that your oasis is probably less than 15 tiles from the meteor biome, and you’re probably going to see more and more meteors fall there over time. I would find a different spot to base.

Label: Strategy
Data:
Im not sure how to upload images onto a reddit comment section, but I could attempt to describe what Ive decided to use as my goto (longterm) base layout after a few years of playing (assuming you have geometric placment mod):

I find a 13x13 tile area, and cobblestone the outmost part of the 13x13 in a ring. I then start at one corner of the base, leave a 2 tile space, and then place 2 cobblestone tiles inwardly towards the center of the 13x13 base area, and I will repeat this step until Ive made it all the way around (this will create 12 sets of 2x2 square areas going along the outer-ring of the base, these will be dedicated to different resources and utilities, with each square having a unique turf, for example: the square dedicated to grass (you can comfortably fit 64 grass tufts in a 2x2 area) will have grassy turf, and the 'crockpot/kitchen square' will have kitchen tiles, this also allows for the usual 2x2 tilled farming plot tile to fit in nicely).

I then 'trim' the inner facing part of these series of squares to create a seperate 'ring' of cobblestone, which will provide the 5x5 tile central part of the base containing all the work stations and chests. I will usually build a workstation in the center of every odd numbered tile of the 5x5, and then 'ring' each station with either 4 or 8 chests. I associate each work station with a different theme, for example: scaled furnace will have chests around it containing either summer related goods or heat/fire related items; the shadow manipulator will have chests contain all the dark magic materials and items; tackle receptical will be ringed by spring gear; think tank boating materials; shadowcraft plinth will be ringed by ruins gear; potters wheel will support all the minerals; alchemy engine will have all the general crafting materials, etc.

In the very center tile of the 13x13 area, I will usually have a firepit, as it throws off enough light to comfortably cover most of the area. Once I acquire ingredients for flingomatics, I craft 4 and place one 4 tiles diagonally in from each corner of the 13x13 base (which will land on the corners of the 'inner' cobblestone trimming), as it covers everything WITHOUT overlapping the central tile containing the firepit (ofc this doesnt matter once you have a scaled furnace and alternative general base lightsources).

If I dont play as a character with alternative base light sources (like wickerbottom or winona), I will usually use toadstools glowcaps by duping enough shroom skins (using napsacks and deconstruction staffs) to make 12 of them, and placing 4 on the cardinal junctions of the inner ring of cobblestones and place the remaining 8 on the junctions of cobble of the four corner 2x2 tiles on the outer-ring. While some areas may appear darker, you wont experience sanity drain and charlie can never get you.

Label: Strategy
Data:
Ofc it's hard

its supposed to be

I reccomend you play either duo or solo in the beggining, it helps get you orietnated.

Are you playing on console? It will be more difficult. On PC, learn how to kite.

Get one player as woodie, make him 'rush lunar island' lots of tutorials. Gives you a great food source, with kelp all year around. Make sure to dry it on a rack first, tho

Base near: A savannah biome, a desert biome, a forest wood - ish biome and a biome with tallbird nests. Set up traps on the rabit holes so you can get bunnies, which you can cook on a fire to get morsels. Hit a beefalo, run, and eventually the rest will stop following you. Hit it 5-6 times, dodge , hit again. Tallbirds, hit 1-2 times, dodge, repeat.

Craft a crockpot. Its amazing for food. Combine 3 plants (Cactus from the desert, or kelp from the lunar island) and a tallbird egg (Found in tallbird nests, big n blue) to make a food which restores 60 health and 150 hunger (AMAZING food source.)

Additional ways to get food is to hammer lots and lots of pig houses, and rebuild them near your base (Not too close) so you can start a 'civil war' ik it sounds weird, js search it up

Find tutorials on how to rush ruins. It's not too hard if you playing w/ 4 people. Beat a boss there, no spoilers for now. The loot makes hunting very easy.

Make sure to mass produce drying racks. They can make meat into jerky. Great way to get longer lasting food, since the jerky is the same thing, just better and can be eaten raw.

Making a honey farm with honey boxes is wise. It allows to make a great healing food, Honey Ham (2 honey, 2 meats)

Make a few farms, here and there. Berry farms are.... alright.

Give roles to players. One focuses ONLY on food, one on exploration, maybe 1 on base building, and 1 on rushing ruins... up to you.

The game is supposed to be hard. That's the point..

Label: Strategy
Data:
to cast spells, you need to collect embers by killing creatures while they are on fire. Butterflies don't work, so bees and spiders are the easiest. Just slap em with your lighter till they die.

Then you can absorb the ember by holding the lighter in your hand and click it.

Once holding embers, click it to cast spells, or much easier- press Z to bring up the spell wheel.

Of all the 'boss unlock' skill perks, Willow's also seems to be the biggest/most powerful/ Easiest to immediately benefit from (like yea other characters like Winona's are strong, but they require you to beat hard bosses again each world before you can benefit from the perk).

Willow just needs to kill bees then she becomes a longrange auto-targeting or AOE wizard assassin as long as she's killed CC or FW atleast once in any world.

so once you get 15 perk points I strongly encourage you to join a game with good players who know how to kill FW/CC so you can have real fun with shadow or lunarflames.

Label: Strategy

Label: Strategy
Data:
Understanding biomes helps a bit too. E.g. if you're looking for beefalo, they only spawn in two places. Big savannah, or mini patch inside mandrake forest.

they do NOT spawn in triple Mctusk setpiece savannah or spawn savannah biome (unless coincidentally big savanah is next to spawn). So if you see a Mctusk in Savannah, you know you've found triple. If you see a Mandrake, well you've found a beefalo for later + you know this ISN"T moon pedestal forest, so you're still looking for moonstone+ scrapyard+ mctusk forest.

Mosaic typically is next to spawn biome, though spawn biome can sometimes be quite large so it can fool you/ you can run some distance in the opposite direction of mosaic without actually having left spawn biome.

Brick roads often lead to pigking, which can help find that faster.

Short fat Cactus is Dfly desert, tall cactus is oasis.

Spiders are always somewhere in swamp, and in those two dense forests.

Much harder to learn, but there is also a pool of 10 'random' biomes, which only 5 get selected each world gen (such as a second birchnut forest). So if you've found 5 of these random biomes and none were a birchnut forest, you know that when you DO find that forest, it's pigking because the second biome doesn't exist this world gen. (Biomes | Don't Starve Wiki | Fandom)

Living logs... yeah it's luck dependent but you have a few ways:

fully exploring those forests (moonstone/mandrake) large chance of finding a 'totally normal tree' that can be cut and dug for 3 logs.

Check cave entrances. if you can see blue mushroom forest or lunar grotto, you're not far from.. lunar grotto that spawns a fat blue mob that can be killed for living logs.

Tree Genocide. tame 6 pigs early morning, run to a dense forest, and cut.

Less luck dependent? play wormwood, self harm.

If you really just want to explore, the best character hands down is woodie. Sure wortox can teleport.. but only to places he's already been.

His weregoose form just costs 3 monster meat and 2 seeds, so find some spiders early and you can make a couple of those.. then you can run the entire map at speed, across water. locate every biome in a day, then start even exploring islands if you want and grab stonefruit, kelp bulbs and monkeytails your first week. Woodie can also dupe living logs at the cost of nightmare fuel with one of his perks.

Label: Strategy
Data:
Moose is absolutely broken for late game bosses actually. He has 90% player damage reduction, which is the only % dmg reduction that works for planar damage AND his skill gets him an extra like 5 or so planar defense. All this with the HP regen makes woodie a BEAST imo.

As a woodie main overall, my favorite thing to do is to have a knobby tree planted by the first autumn, it’ll be grown by spring and then the base has full lightning protection, 30% rain prot, AND everything in its shade is immune to overheating AND wildfires (you probably still want a flingo for fire hounds though learned this the hard way)

Label: Strategy
Data: Each crop has a happiness stat. Being talked to, season, location as well as having enough food & water influences their happiness. Happy crops give seeds back. By location I mean growing crops of the same type together (4 or more), not crowding too many crops in one area(no more than 9 per tile) & not having any weeds or debris nearby. You can check how happy each plant is with a garden hat. You can also use the hat to research crops & read your garden scrapbook which shows you the specific wants of each crop that you’ve researched.

Label: Strategy

Label: Strategy
Data:
Bee boxes and flowers. That's it. They are so overturned it's hard not to overproduce with them. Done correctly, a dozen bee boxes can produce 100-200 honey every day in summer.

If your bee boxes aren't producing as much as you'd like, you almost certainly don't have enough flowers nearby. With not enough flowers, more bee boxes will slow down production not speed it up.

Generally it is better to build bee boxes in a line, not in a clump. It helps to spread the bees out which reduces flower competition. It also helps with harvesting, since it is easier to harvest a bunch of boxes in one pass, wasting less bee time.

When harvesting a row of bee boxes, harvest every other box. This allows you to run just far enough from the bees from the first box to harvest again without managing the bee aggro. When you reach the end of the row, continue to run past the final box until all the bees deaggro because you got too far away. Then run back along the row harvesting the boxes you skipped in the same manner. Bonus points for having cobblestone along your row of boxes to speed up harvest passes.

If you do it right and have enough flowers, you should be able to harvest honey nonstop during the daytime. The reason for doing passes along the bee boxes like that is to maximize the time bees are out of the box and not fighting you so they can pollenate more flowers in a day.

For producing flowers, I recommend using homeless bees instead of planting them all yourself. Set up all your bee boxes but no flowers. Place a lureplant in the middle of your bee bee farm. Capture 10-20 butterflies but don't plant them yet. Once the eye plants spawn, allow them to catch a bunch of bees. Harvest the leafy meat to retract the eye plants. Plant the butterflies all together near the center of be bee boxes where the lureplant is, then kill the lureplant. This will puke out all the bees it caught as homeless bees.

Homeless bees don't have a bee box or hive to store honey in. Once they visit enough flowers to produce a honey they will go to a nearby empty space on the ground and make a new flower instead.

Label: Strategy
Data:
I think if you're stuggling with this, then just learning how to easily take out spider nests in your best bet.

First off, make sure it's day time. Dusk or Night has spiders out of their nest that will all swarm you if you kill any. Step on the web so a small group of spiders come out. Tier 1 nests will have 1, 2 for tier 2, 3 for tier 3. Stay on the edge of the web until the spider walks up to you to attack, then back away like 1-2 tiles from the edge of the web, hold F. It won't trigger more spiders to come. Repeat 3 times (doesn't matter nest size). Then walk up to the nest and kill it for bonus silk. Tier 2 and tier 3 nests will spawn one or two spider warriors respectively. You can place down a simple trap and lure it in for a simple kill. Or if you have moderate armor and weapons, it will only get off like one attack, just tank+spank.

Label: Strategy
Data:
Believe me, there are tons of food in this game you just need to know where.

First thing you need to learn is how to use crockpot. Crockpot most common recipe is meatball. It’s 1 meat + 3 edibles (ice, berry, carrot etc) for meatball. The best part about meatball you can use monster meat (just make sure not use more than 1 in your recipe)

Second thing is how to fight, tons of things drop meat in this game. Spiders, marotters, tallbirds, pigs. Just have one person playing wigfrid and make battle helm for everyone to fight. Character like Wendy is really good at killing spiders and frogs.

Third is utilize your time better, you should hold a torch and continue to explore at night time instead of returning to base, the more resource you find the easier it is to feed yourself.

Label: Strategy
Data:
Don't rely on starting food sources if you can help it, especially with more than 2 people. If you can, try to memorize that the average character loses around 75 hunger points a day. This is around 6 carrots. You will find it MUCH easier if you use a crock pot to prepare meals that refill hunger better than plain items.

I have been getting into farming in DST solo recently, and when you make a habit of planting every seed you see (birds drop them when you scare them away quite often) in a 4x4 plot (one whole digamajig), you will find yourself with more food than you know what to do with. You don't even have to water or fertilize them, they'll do their own thing usually. That being said... You will want to water and fertilize atleast a little, to give you faster crop growth.

Little things I do for food in solo that also goes a long way:

Make atleast 3 drying racks. Throw any meat/kelp/frog legs onto it. It's good for emergencies and long journies.

Use a shovel/have a werebeaver woodie to dig up berry bushes to plant at your base (not ALL of them though, a little secret is that you don't need to fertilize a berry bush that you've never moved!!)

Memorize how much meat you get from a mob. Beefalo are a good source of big meat in a bad situation. Note that big meat, monster meat, and a morsel are very different. Usually, you will want big meat.

Keep a bee box. Honey can be really useful for healing and feeding.

Don't be shy with making multiple ice boxes and crock pots. A common setup is 6 crock pots around 1 Ice box.

Make a habit of mining ice. You can keep it in an ice box (I think forever) and use it as filler for a lot of crock pot recipes.

Keep a bird in a cage. Feed it any meat and it'll produce eggs. Any crop and it'll produce seeds.

Keep a fishing rod handy for when there's NO good food around. Fresh water fish are more of a hassle to get during winter though.

Stone fruit trees!! You get them from Lunar Island. If you want to get this early, have a woodie use weregoose and you could probably get some on first day.

Use the craft pot client mod and a mod called Insight to know exactly how much hunger you're restoring from a food. It's MUCH easier than counting and remembering what every food does. That way you and your friends can kinda gage how efficient a crock pot recipe will be vs how much it takes to cook.

It helps to have too many options for food. What's available will change during seasons and this game is meant to be hard, especially in groups.

Label: Strategy
Data: I wouldn't waste time digging up berry bushes like others have said. They don't grow in winter, summer they wilt. That's two season it's down. Number #1 priority is a spider den for monster meat, silk and glands. Kill them all, destroy the tier 3 nest and relocate closer to base. Use the extra monster meat and use a bird cage to turn into eggs. If you have too many eggs, you can turn into gold with pig king. You can never have too much monster meat, silk or glands. Number #2 is pig houses. Hammer as many on the map as you can and make a pig area. You can use the monster meat to make them friendly then start a pig war. All the big meat and pig skin you desire. Number #3 for me is bee boxes, this is something you can do later on, during summer/autumn. Use the silk to make bug nets and catch regular bees. Then destroy the angry bee hives first to make bee boxes. If not, just destroy any bee hives for bee boxes. Stock up on honey especially during summer when the days are long. Only season it's down is winter. Number #4 is find the ponds for easy frog legs. As a Wigfrid main, I live for easy frog legs. I can dodge them easily or even tank a hit. But if you have tons of twigs and grass, use regular traps 10-20 depending on how many ponds. That's just easy frog legs which is equivalent to a morsel. Then you can make Bacon n Eggs, Honey Ham, Honey Nuggets or Pierogi (just needs one mushroom/veggie).

Label: Strategy
Data:
I’ve honestly never really used a beefalo for actual utility rather than just taming one and starting a herd where I want it. But I would assume if you’re going to fight with it, you’d use whatever one other character ride for battle.

For healing, basically you want compost wraps and a bat bat during battle, with sleeping in a tent as the primary way to heal when not actively in combat. Don’t be afraid to use the crap out of that tent (also cut off your arm for living logs first and then heal). Wormwood can eat anything really, food is never an issue. Plant red mushroom caps inside a cave for quick on demand food that won’t ever spoil if not picked. If you have seeds to plant, even eat raw meat.

You can also make honey poultice. Be aware though they take longer to heal with that eating food that would heal any other character. It makes it harder to use mid combat, need to create some space and have time to use it.

Kiting is absolutely essential. Taking the moon shroom skill is great for putting things to sleep (moon shrooms are also good food for him too).

Personally, with a bit of experience and a good set up, all random npcs / enemies will not be a problem. Boss fights end up being more difficult, as you can’t just power through with high healing food. (And I don’t know how you fight bosses that can’t be easily kited or tricked into fighting something else).

Label: Strategy
Data:
Green amulets halves the cost crafting something by half while green staves is like "hammering" something but you get 100% of materials back if is at 100% durability and that percentage drops depending of how much you used the item.

For example: if you craft, and imaginary armor for the example, that costs 10 boards with the amulet it costs you 5 boards. When you decontruc it you get the 10 boards back if is at 100% durability. Let's say you use it until it reaches 71%, then you get 7 boards when deconstructed. Also, if you try to desconstruc an item with a recipe that contains a single piece of a ingredient, let's say pan flute requiring just 1 mandrake, you get that ingredient back no matter the durability because you get at least 1 ingredient of each back when is at low durability so a 10% mandrake drops 1 mandrake, 1 reed and 1 rope when deconstructed the same way the imaginary armor of the previous example will give 1 board at <10% durability

Take into account that you cant dupe gems. Every gem is destroyed when you try to desconstruc an item that contains them except for the moon caller staff for some reason

Label: Strategy
Data:
Spring is pretty chill compared to winter and summer— just make some rain gear and a lightning rod and you’ll be good! If you beat deerclops def make the eyebrella. Oh and watch out for frog rain lolol.

To prepare for summer either get a watering can or ice flinger thing (I forget what it’s called) cuz stuff will kinda just catch on fire when it feels like it. And if your character says anything about tremors just run away from your base ASAP 😅

Also congrats for passing winter!! That’s no easy task

Label: Strategy
Data:
Crab king tbh is not that difficult, having Abigail really helps. My best bet is, put pearls pearl in, the rest purple (claws). Because you can entirely avoid attacking claws, with drift wood paddle you are way faster then them. Have a fresh hambat, stock up on pierogis, armor, have a reliable light source, have important stuff in a chest on boat so they dont get flinged off by cannonballs. Stock up about 10 boards and 10 leak repairs. Weather pain helps, tho not obligational. Ofc buffs for abigail.

Fight starts you paddle away from claws, go for first tower, pass by it and give it 2 knocks with bat it should knock it down (take care to never paddle directly towards to not ram the tower). Repeat until no towers left, lead claws far away, make a U turn to crab king, hammer him and keep avoidi claws. As soon as crab king cast waves you start paddling towards it. Forms ice. Main goal here is to 1. stop him from healing by doing damage (Abigail or Weather pane) 2. Break barrier to hammer crab king. Attack guards only if neccessary. Get back on boat safely before ice breaks.

Rinse and repeat until done. Weather pane is great tool to stop crab king from healing when on pc (console aim for weather pane is bad).

If you get annoyed enough gunpowder will do too eventually. Most important here is having the supplies for boat repairs and leak fixes aswell light and pierogies, have a bunch of driftwood ready for making more oars on the go. And navigating the boat via paddling. I reccommend disconnecting the game at crab king so you can reroll back to thr fight start every time it goes sideways.

Good luck, hope you succeed.

Label: Strategy
Data:
So, I haven’t used a guide, but I’m in the midst of introducing my friend to the game, and I think the reason the guides are micromanagey is because there are kinda only two ways to guide people in this game. A) Barely guide, let people make their own mistakes and die a lot. B) Micromanage.

The game wants you to die. It wants you to learn through making mistakes. Before DST, death meant game over, so you were supposed to learn incrementally what will kill you, and what you must do to survive it. So, honestly? That’s what I recommend. Jump on, make your own mistakes.

If you want to learn like I learned, do that + have the wiki available to look through when you encounter something you’re curious about.

The thing is that there is kind of an order you have to do things in to survive, at least to start. If you fail to do these things in their order, you’ll get caught in a loop of dying. (For example, if you don’t get gold and make a science machine early enough, you’re not making an alchemy engine, so you’re not making the things you need to survive winter).

The order is roughly:

Gather materials for torches and campfires and make them before night.

Gather materials for science machine and make one, (make a shovel and backpack, and anything else you want, then hammer it down for materials.)

Find beefalo or pig king.

Gather materials for alchemy engine.

Make base.

Gather materials for miner’s hat and make one.

Gather materials to warm you up in winter.

Then it proceeds like that, going “gather materials for the next threat or season” as you continue.

If the above counts as a guide and you find it non micromanagey, I could try and make one for a full year. If you find that micromanagey, you’re not going to find one that isn’t.

Label: Strategy
Data:
Here's a newish player friendly list:

0. I put a "zero" starting point because killing Dragonfly on your first autumn is fairly hard but definitely doable in a group. This is optional but will definitely help you a lot for survival.

Deerclops (winter, year 1)

Eye of Terror (winter, year 1)

Moose Goose (spring, year 1)

Antlion (summer, year 1)

Dragonfly (if you did not do it in the first autumn [autumn, year 2])

Bearger (autumn, year 2)

I tend to do whichever boss I want to battle after this list. Defeating these bosses secures my survival for the coming years. I tend to do Ancient Guardian, Bee Queen, and Klaus soon after Bearger because of their very helpful drops.

Label: Strategy
Data:
You can do both, of course, but you wanted a simple progression path. Both paths lead to the endgame, for the surface and caves respectively and depending on your characters, you might have different gear requirements that favor one path over the other (for example, Wigfrid, Winona and Wormwood love the lunar path, Wurt, Wortox and Maxwell the shadow path, etc).

But yeah, you can do both paths at the same time. My usual route for that is:

- Ruins + Ancient Guardian
- Moon Stone event on a full moon (day 11 if you're fast)
- Archives
- Nightmare Werepig on the way back (make sure he smashes the pillars in his second phase for Dreadstone!)
- Pearl's tasks (you can finish them all on day 21-22, look up a guide)
- Lunar altars, Crab King and Moonstorm
- Celestial Champion kill (important, wait for a full new day to come before you mine CC's remains!)
- Unlock lunar rifts by giving Wagstaff an Enlightened Shard (drops after you kill CC)
- You will have a full day of new moon after you mine CC, so go and do Shadow Pieces
- Wait for the rifts, then craft Brightshade items
- Spilagmites for fossils
- Use the Brightshade staff to trivialize Ancient Fuelweaver
- Give Charlie 5 Dreadstone from the Nightmare Werepig to unlock Shadow Rifts

Done. This way, you will have unlocked both endgame states for the world and have access to all the gear. If done correctly, you will finish this by the end of your first spring, though you need to have a bit of RNG luck and some good game knowledge. Happy hunting!

Label: Strategy
Data:
If you're freshly starting out the game, just survive. Surviving a year is the most important part and teaches you the basic gameplay loop. Just going to the ruins and killing Ancient Guardian should be your big goal.

If you're more advanced, you need to make up your mind whether to do the lunar or shadow progression. The choice depends mostly on your picked characters and affinities.

For lunar:

- Ruins + Ancient Guardian
- Moon Stone event (Full Moon)
- Ancient Archives
- Pearl's tasks
- Lunar altars
- Crab King
- Moonstorm event
- Celestial Champion

For shadow:

- Ruins + Ancient Guardian
- Nightmare Werepig
- Shadow Pieces (New Moon)
- Spilagmites for 8 fossils
- Ancient Fuelweaver

Beyond that and the seasonal bosses, all else is optional, so just beat whatever boss has decent loot for you all. Bee Queen for Bundling Wraps is btw almost always a very, very good idea.

Label: Strategy
Data: So first, like another person said, boat patches, definitely would need them, bumpers (any kind would work) maybe if you're up for getting food on the fly to save food it would be best to try and fish and make a ton fishing bin, also, tip, at night davy jones will try and unpatch boat patches, get rid of the anchor, and unwind the sail, you can step on their face to get rid of them (I think three times. Label: Strategy
Data:
For Skills, I run the following Skills:
- Alpha Tinkering I and II (Reason below.)
- Beta Tinkering I (Same as below.)
- Gamma Tinkering I (I find that this combination of Tinkering Perks gives a massive amount of utility in Circuits for every purpose I could need.)
- Off By One (This one is darn-near essential for most Circuit builds.)
- Right To Modify (Reason below.)
- Watts Up (With the buffs these two perks got just before release, Right to Modify and Watts Up are REALLY HARD to not take. Being able to hotswap Circuits for less damage and also charge faster with less consumption when unplugging are both powerful tools when you're setting up, and are very handy for getting rolling lategame faster. They also let you abuse Inhabited Machine II more often, roughly within half a day.)
- Cold Standby I (Required for any Chassis Perks.)
- Cold Standby I, II, and III, and Remote Transfer (Remote Transfer requires all 3, but having up to 4 Chassis is handy regardless.)
- Inhabited Machine I (Being able to self-revive is fantastic, and pairs directly with Inhabited Machine II.)
- Inhabited Machine II (As mentioned above, in particular, the ability to literally just say "Nah, I don't really feel like it" when you die is an amazing utility. Just be sure to keep a safety torch on hand for if you rez in the dark.)
- Transport I and II (Drew is amazing utility. A chest that can travel across the world for the cost of a price trade of a Board for a Doodad and 3 inventory slots. Absolutely glorious.)

Now for my Circuits, I usually run the following:
Alpha:
- Super-Hardy, Super-Processing/Beanbooster, Super-Gastrogain, [Flex slot] (One of each puts WX at 200 of each stat, which is not bad in the slightest. Additionally, Off By One allows you to add a basic Alpha Circuit to boost a stat to 240.)
Beta:
- Optoelectronic Circuit (Night vision. 'Nuff said. With Beta Tinkering I, the filter becomes almost normal vision, to the point I often lose track of what time of day it is.)
- Super-Acceleration Circuit (25% extra speed is nothing to scoff at.)
- Flex Slot (Usually I just put a Rangebooster in here, since the Optoelectronic Circuit makes a Super-Illumination Circuit pointless, but it can also be handy for more collaborative efforts.)
Gamma:
- Sonic-Invoker Circuit (With Gamma Tinkering I, this Circuit becomes REALLY potent. Being able to force-fear any non-boss creature that draws near is... It's just great.)
- Redigestion Circuit x2 (Turning Rot and Spoiled foods into Nutribricks via Gamma Tinkering I is a pretty sweet benefit for basically no cost. Alternatively, Spin Cycle could be used, but there's no 1-slot Gamma Circuit yet, so...

Maybe the game isnt for you if you need to die on that hill, justifying how bad the game is because you died once.

If you require handholding to figure stuff out then this game aint for you.
You can watch a beginner tutorial, quick guide on youtube and put in the bare minimum effort yourself or not, refund your 2 bucks and miss out on a damn good game.
